rules.title: Vowel Alternation (Чергування голосних) in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, certain vowels change when a word's syllable structure changes between cases or forms. The most common alternations are о↔і and е↔і, typically seen when a closed syllable (with і) becomes open (with о or е) in forms like the locative case.

examples.note: The word 'стіл' changes to 'стол' in the locative because the syllable becomes open.
'кіт' becomes 'кот' in the locative for the same reason.
'піч' changes 'і' to 'е' in the locative when the syllable opens.

exercises.prompt: Write the locative form (meaning 'on the table') of the word 'стіл'.
Write the locative form (meaning 'on the stove') of the word 'піч'.

rules.title: Mastering the Soft Sign (М'який знак)
rules.explanation: The soft sign (ь) softens the preceding consonant. Unlike English, it is a distinct letter that changes pronunciation. Common mistakes include omitting it where needed or adding it incorrectly.

examples.note: Without the soft sign, 'л' is hard; with it, 'л' becomes soft, which is essential for the correct pronunciation of 'teacher'.
The soft sign changes 'н' from hard to soft, altering the meaning to 'day'.
Similarly, 'соль' means 'salt' with a soft 'л', while 'сол' would be a different (non-existent) word.

exercises.prompt: Add the soft sign (ь) to make the word for 'horse': кін →
Choose the correct Ukrainian word for 'day': (a) ден  (b) день
Write the correct Ukrainian word for 'salt' (ending with soft sign):

rules.title: Common Ukrainian Stress (Наголос) Mistakes
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, stress placement can change meaning or sound unnatural if wrong. Many learners incorrectly stress the first syllable of common words like 'один' or 'була'. The stress is not predictable, so memorize the correct syllable.

examples.note: The stress on the first syllable (o) is incorrect; it should be on the second syllable (i).
For the feminine past tense verb 'to be', stress falls on the final syllable (a), not the first.
The nationality adjective is stressed on the second syllable (ї), not the first (a).

exercises.prompt: Place the stress correctly in the word: один
Place the stress correctly in the word: була
Place the stress correctly in the word: українська

rules.title: Genitive Case After Negation
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, when a verb is negated (e.g., немає, не було, не бачу), the object must be in the genitive case. English speakers often mistakenly use the nominative form. Focus on changing the ending of the noun.

examples.note: 'стіл' (nominative) → 'стола' (genitive)
'книжка' (nominative) → 'книжки' (genitive)

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct genitive form: 'Немає ______ (друг)'
Correct the sentence: 'Я не маю час.'

rules.title: Soft Consonants (М'які приголосні) in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, consonants can be 'hard' (non-palatalized) or 'soft' (palatalized). Soft consonants are pronounced with the middle of the tongue raised toward the hard palate, similar to the 'ny' in 'canyon' or 'ly' in 'million'. English speakers often omit this softening, which can change the meaning of words. Pay attention to the soft sign (ь) and certain vowels (я, є, і, ї, ю) that indicate softness.

examples.note: The soft sign (ь) softens the final 't', changing 'mat' (a mat) to 'mother'.
The vowel 'ю' makes the preceding 'л' soft, turning 'bow/onion' into 'hatch'.
The soft sign softens the 'н', distinguishing 'son' from 'blue' (feminine color).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'mother' in Ukrainian, ensuring the final 't' is soft?
Write the Ukrainian word for 'hatch' (e.g., a door hatch) with a soft 'l'.
Write the Ukrainian word for 'blue' (feminine), making sure it differs from 'son'.

rules.title: No Diphthongs in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: Ukrainian does not have diphthongs like English. Every vowel is pronounced clearly and separately. When you see two vowel letters in a row, say each one distinctly without gliding. Common mistakes include blending 'ау' into 'au' as in 'cow', or 'ой' into 'oy'.

examples.note: Say 'pa-u-za' (three syllables), not 'pow-za' as in English 'pause'.
Pronounce 'klo-un' (two syllables), not 'klown'.

exercises.prompt: Write the Ukrainian word 'пауза' with hyphens to show correct syllable division.
Which syllable division is correct for 'клоун'? Write it with hyphens.
Correct the English-style mispronunciation 'pow-za' by writing the correct Ukrainian word.

rules.title: Perfective vs Imperfective Aspect in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, every verb has two aspects: imperfective (for ongoing, habitual, or incomplete actions) and perfective (for completed, one-time, or achieved actions). English speakers often confuse them because English does not mark aspect as strictly. Use imperfective with actions that are repeated or in progress, and perfective when the action is finished or has a clear result.

examples.note: Use perfective 'прочитав' to show that you finished reading the book yesterday, not just that you were reading it.
Use imperfective 'купував' for a repeated/habitual action like buying bread every day.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct past-tense verb: I ______ (read) this book yesterday (completed action). Use perfective.
Fill in the blank with the correct past-tense verb: He ______ (buy) bread every morning last year (habitual). Use imperfective.
Fill in the blank with the correct present-tense verb: I ______ (write) a letter right now (ongoing). Use imperfective.

rules.title: Genitive vs Accusative After Negation
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, when a verb is negated (using 'не'), the direct object typically changes from the accusative case to the genitive case. This rule applies to most transitive verbs. For example, 'Я читаю книгу' (I read a book) becomes 'Я не читаю книги' (I don't read a book).

examples.note: After 'не бачу', the object should be in genitive: 'стола' instead of accusative 'стіл'.
After 'не купила', use genitive plural 'квітів' instead of accusative 'квіти'.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I don't have a sister.' (use 'мати' - to have, 'сестра' - sister)
Translate: 'He doesn't drink water.' (use 'пити' - to drink, 'вода' - water)

rules.title: Prepositions в, на, у: When to Use Which
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, в and у both mean 'in' or 'at', but в is used before consonants and у before vowels for smooth pronunciation. The preposition на means 'on' (surface), 'at' (events or open areas like beach, market) or 'by' (means of transport). A common mistake is using на instead of в for enclosed spaces or institutions (e.g., offices, universities).

examples.note: Use в for being inside a building or room.
Use на for open areas like beach, stadium, or market.
Use в for educational institutions, hospitals, etc.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct preposition (в, у, or на): I am going to the cinema.
Fill in the blank: She is at the market.
Fill in the blank: He lives in the city.

rules.title: Numeral Agreement with Nouns
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, the noun form changes depending on the numeral. After 2, 3, and 4, masculine inanimate nouns use the nominative plural (e.g., столи), while feminine and neuter nouns use the genitive singular (e.g., книги, вікна). For 5 and above, all nouns require the genitive plural (e.g., столів, книг). A common mistake is using the wrong case after these numbers.

examples.note: With masculine inanimate nouns like стіл, use nominative plural after 2,3,4.
After 5 and above, all nouns take genitive plural.
With feminine nouns like книга, use genitive singular after 2,3,4.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct form for 'two tables' (стіл).
Fill in the correct form for 'five books' (книга).
Fill in the correct form for 'three windows' (вікно).

rules.topic: Directional vs multidirectional verbs of motion
rules.title: Directional vs Multidirectional Verbs of Motion in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: Ukrainian distinguishes between directional verbs (one-way, specific direction at a specific time) and multidirectional verbs (repeated, habitual, or back-and-forth motion). English speakers often mistakenly use the directional form for repeated actions. Learn the pairs: іти (to go on foot, directional) vs ходити (multidirectional), їхати (to go by vehicle, directional) vs їздити (multidirectional).

examples.wrong: Я йду до магазину кожен день.
examples.right: Я ходжу до магазину кожен день.
examples.note: Use the multidirectional 'ходжу' for a habitual action (every day).
Use 'їздить' for repeated trips (every summer).
This is correct! 'Йдемо' is directional and appropriate for a specific current action.

exercises.prompt: Complete: Every morning I (go) to work on foot. (habitual)
Complete: This weekend we (go) to Lviv by train. (one-time specific)
exercises.answer: Цього вікенду ми їдемо до Львова поїздом.

exercises.prompt: Complete: He (go) to the gym three times a week. (repeated)
exercises.answer: Він ходить до спортзалу тричі на тиждень.

rules.title: Possessive Pronouns: Gender and Number Agreement
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, possessive pronouns must agree in gender and number with the noun they modify (the owned object), not with the owner. A common mistake is using the same form for all nouns, but in fact 'мій' (masculine), 'моя' (feminine), 'моє' (neuter), and 'мої' (plural) are distinct forms.

examples.note: 'Стіл' is masculine, so use 'мій'.
'Книга' is feminine, so use 'моя'.
'Друзі' is plural, so use 'мої'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct possessive form of 'my': Це ______ ручка. (Ручка is feminine.)
Fill in the correct possessive form of 'my': Це ______ місто. (Місто is neuter.)
Fill in the correct possessive form of 'my': Це ______ друзі. (Plural.)

rules.title: Using 'би' in Conditional Mood
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, the conditional mood is formed by adding the particle 'би' (or 'б' after vowels) to a past tense verb. A common mistake is using 'би' with present or future tense verbs instead of the past tense. Always use the past tense form of the verb to express a hypothetical situation.

examples.note: 'хочу' is present tense; use past tense 'хотів'.
'піде' is future tense; use past tense 'пішов'.
'може' is present tense; use past 'могла' and shorten 'би' to 'б' after the vowel.

exercises.prompt: Correct this sentence: 'Я буду би радий.'
Correct this sentence: 'Ти зробиш би це?'
Correct this sentence: 'Ми хочемо би піти.'

rules.title: Passive Voice with -ся Verbs: Common Mistakes
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, -ся verbs can express passive meaning in the present tense (e.g., книга читається – the book is being read). However, English learners often mistakenly use -ся for past or completed actions, where you should use the auxiliary бути + passive participle (e.g., книга була прочитана – the book was read). The -ся form in past tense usually indicates a reflexive or spontaneous action, not a passive with an agent.

examples.note: For past passive, use 'був/була/було/були + past passive participle', not -ся.
-ся verbs in past tense often mean 'got done by itself' – use the participle for a clear passive meaning.

examples.wrong: Двері відчинилися (коли я хотів сказати, що їх відчинили).
examples.note: If an agent performed the action, avoid -ся; use 'бути + participle' or the active voice.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'The window was opened by the wind.' (Use correct passive form.)
Correct this sentence: 'Завдання зробилося легко.' (Change to proper passive for a completed action.)
How would you say 'The letter is being written right now' (present passive)?

rules.title: Avoiding Active Participles in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: Active participles (present and past) exist in Ukrainian but are very rare in everyday speech. English speakers often mistakenly use forms like 'читаючий' (reading) or 'написавший' (having written). Instead, Ukrainians naturally use relative clauses with 'який' (who/which) plus a verb. Stick to 'який + verb' for clear, natural communication.

examples.note: Replace 'читаючий' with 'який читає' (who reads).
Past active participle 'написавший' is bookish; use 'який написав' (who wrote).
Present active participle 'співаюча' sounds unnatural; use 'яка співає'.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'the girl who is dancing' using a relative clause.
Correct the sentence: 'хлопчик, граючий у футбол'.
How would you say 'the writer who created this book' (past action) naturally?

rules.title: Using Ukrainian Gerunds (Дієприслівники) Correctly
rules.explanation: Ukrainian gerunds (дієприслівники) express an action that happens at the same time as or before the main verb. They are not used like English '-ing' forms after prepositions. Instead, choose the correct form: use the simultaneous gerund (e.g., читаючи) for actions happening together, and the past gerund (e.g., прочитавши) for actions completed before the main verb.

examples.note: Use the past gerund купивши (having bought) instead of the infinitive щоб купити (in order to buy) when the buying happens before leaving.
Use the simultaneous gerund співаючи (while singing) to show two actions at once, avoiding the conjunction.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'Having read the book, she went to sleep.' (use past gerund of читати)
Translate: 'He listens to music while working.' (use simultaneous gerund of працювати)

rules.title: Comparative and Superlative Degrees: Synthetic vs. Analytic Forms
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, most adjectives form the comparative by adding -іший or -ший (synthetic), and the superlative by adding най- to that form. A common mistake is using the analytic forms 'більш' (more) or 'найбільш' (most) with adjectives that already have a synthetic form. Use analytic forms only when the adjective lacks a synthetic comparative (e.g., borrowed words) or for emphasis.

examples.note: Use the synthetic form -іший for 'цікавий' (interesting).
For superlative, add най- to the synthetic comparative form.
Adjectives like 'важливий' (important) also take synthetic -іший.

exercises.prompt: Correct the sentence: 'Ця книга більш цікава.'
Correct the sentence: 'Він найбільш розумний.'
Write the comparative form of 'сильний' in this sentence: 'Мій брат _____ (сильний) за мене.'

rules.title: Double Negation with Ні- Prefixes: Using Не for Correct Negation
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, words with the negative prefix ні- (e.g., ніхто, ніщо, ніколи) must always be followed by the negative particle не before the verb. This double negation is grammatically required, unlike in English where only one negative is used. Omitting не is a common mistake for English learners.

examples.note: The word ніхто needs an additional не before the verb.
Always use не with нічого to make a correct negative sentence.
With ніколи (never), the verb must have не as well.

exercises.prompt: Translate into Ukrainian: 'Nobody knows.'
Correct the mistake: 'Ми ніде ходили.'
Translate: 'I never eat meat.'

rules.title: Common Mistake: Conjunctions і, та, а, але
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, і and та both mean 'and' (і is more common; та is slightly more informal or used for variety). Use а to contrast two different facts (like 'while' or 'and on the other hand'), and але for a direct 'but' (strong opposition). A common mistake is using а where але is needed, or using і where а is required.

examples.wrong: Я люблю каву, а вона не любить.
examples.right: Я люблю каву, але вона не любить.
examples.note: Use але for a clear contradiction (she does not like it). Using а here sounds incomplete or unnatural.

examples.wrong: Він читає книгу, і вона дивиться фільм.
examples.right: Він читає книгу, а вона дивиться фільм.
examples.note: Use а to contrast two different actions happening at the same time (he reads, while she watches).

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct conjunction: 'I wanted to go, ___ I was tired.'
Fill in: 'She likes tea, ___ he prefers coffee.' (contrast, not strong opposition)

rules.title: Pronouncing the Ukrainian Letter Щ
rules.explanation: The letter щ represents a single, soft, prolonged 'sh' sound, similar to the 'sh' in 'sheep' but with your tongue raised higher and held longer. It is not a combination of 'sh' and 'ch' (like 'shch') – that is a common mistake for English speakers. Practice making a smooth, hissing sound without separating it into two parts.

examples.note: ‘щука’ (pike) – pronounce as a soft, long ‘shoo-ka’, not ‘shchoo-ka’.
‘дощ’ (rain) – the final sound is a soft, extended ‘sh’, not ‘shch’.
‘щітка’ (brush) – keep the ‘sh’ sound continuous and soft, avoiding a break into ‘sh’+‘ch’.

exercises.prompt: Spell the Ukrainian word for ‘rain’ with the letter щ.
Write the Ukrainian word for ‘pike’ that uses the letter щ.
How do you spell the Ukrainian word for ‘brush’ with the letter щ?

rules.title: Hard vs Soft Adjective Endings in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, adjectives have hard or soft endings depending on the final consonant of the stem. For masculine nominative, hard adjectives end in -ий (e.g., добрий) and soft adjectives in -ій (e.g., синій). For feminine nominative, hard endings use -а (e.g., велика) and soft endings use -я (e.g., синя). A common mistake is using the wrong ending, such as writing -ий after a soft stem or -а after a soft stem.

examples.note: The stem 'син' ends in a soft 'н', so the masculine form takes the soft ending -ій, not -ий.
For feminine, a soft stem like 'син' requires the ending -я, not -а.

exercises.prompt: Write the correct masculine nominative form of 'blue' (based on stem син-):
Write the correct feminine nominative form of 'blue' (based on stem син-):
Write the correct masculine nominative form of 'good' (based on stem добр-):

rules.title: Using the Vocative Case in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, when you address someone directly (calling their name or title), you must change the noun into the vocative case. English speakers often make the mistake of using the nominative form instead. For masculine names ending in a consonant, add -e or -u; for feminine names ending in -a, change the -a to -o.

examples.note: Masculine name ending in consonant: add -e.
Feminine name ending in -a: change to -o.
For masculine titles like 'професор', also add -e.

exercises.prompt: Address your friend Andriy (masculine) in a sentence: 'Hello, Andriy!'
Call your sister Olena: 'Olena, come here!'
Say 'Thank you, teacher (пані вчителька)' (feminine title).

rules.title: The Letter ґ vs г: A Common Ukrainian Mistake
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, 'г' represents a soft 'h' sound (like English 'hello'), while 'ґ' represents a hard 'g' sound (like English 'go'). Many learners mistakenly use 'г' where 'ґ' is needed, especially in words borrowed from other languages or in native Ukrainian words with a hard 'g' sound. Remember: 'ґ' is always pronounced as a hard 'g', and it appears in specific words like ґанок, ґудзик, and ґрати.

examples.note: Means 'button'. The hard 'g' sound requires ґ.
Means 'porch' or 'stoop'. A common word where ґ is essential.
Means 'needle'. The sequence 'нґ' has a distinct hard g sound.

exercises.prompt: Write the Ukrainian word for 'hook' (a hard g sound).
Write the Ukrainian word for 'lattice' or 'grille' (hard g).
Write the Ukrainian word for 'button' (hard g).

rules.title: Verbal Prefixes and Aspect in Ukrainian
rules.explanation: In Ukrainian, verbs have two aspects: imperfective (ongoing or repeated actions) and perfective (completed actions). Adding a prefix to an imperfective verb often creates a perfective verb, but not all prefixes work the same way—some also change the meaning. Common prefixes that indicate completion include про- (through/completely), на- (on/result), and за- (begin/complete). Avoid using an imperfective verb when you mean a finished action.

examples.note: Use 'прочитати' (perfective) to emphasize that you finished reading the book.
Use 'написати' (perfective) to show that she completed writing the letter.
Use 'зробити' (perfective) to indicate the homework is finished.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence with the correct perfective form of 'робити' (to do): Я _____ домашнє завдання. (I did/finished my homework.)
Fill in the blank: Він _____ сніданок. (He ate breakfast – completed action.) Use the perfective form of 'їсти' (to eat).
Choose the correct verb: Вчора ми _____ (дивитися / подивитися) фільм. (Yesterday we watched a movie – finished action.)
